---
title: "How To Prepare For Class"
---

This page gives a weekly rhythm for using the course materials. Because the course normally meets twice per week, each weekly module has two live sessions, one book chapter, one slide deck, and one notebook. It does not add any graded work. Course grading, assessment dates, and formal policies are governed by the [syllabus](syllabus.qmd).

The course has several resources because they do different jobs:

| Resource | Main Role |
|---|---|
| [Weekly roadmap](classes.qmd) | Find the week, two-session plan, slides, notebook, book chapter, quiz timing, and project milestones. |
| [Book](book/index.qmd) | Read the slower explanation and connect the mathematics to the course arc. |
| [Slides](classes.qmd) | Follow the live class path: questions, derivations, examples, and discussion. |
| [Notebooks](notebooks/index.qmd) | Change parameters, run experiments, and see what the model does. |
| [Practice](practice.qmd) | Keep short ungraded notes about evidence, assumptions, and failure cases. |
| [Quizzes](quizzes.qmd) | Check quiz timing, permitted tools, and preparation focus. |
| [Project](project/index.qmd) | Prepare the modeling project, report, LLM-use statement, and oral defense. |

## Weekly Rhythm

| Moment | What To Do | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Before Session 1 | Open the [weekly roadmap](classes.qmd). Read enough of the book chapter to recognize the main objects and formulas. Skim the beginning of the weekly slide deck. | One question you want the week to answer. |
| During Session 1 | Use the first part of the slides for concepts, derivations, and guided examples. Track the unknown image, data, forward process, prior, and possible failure. | Annotated notes around the main model. |
| Between Sessions | Revisit the main model and identify what the data support and what the assumptions add. | One question or uncertainty to bring to Session 2. |
| During Session 2 | Use the second part of the slides for consequences, computation, notebook discussion, quizzes, or project work. | A clearer link between model, algorithm, and evidence. |
| After Session 2 | Run the notebook, preferably in Colab if you do not want a local setup. Change at least one important parameter. | A four-thread note about measurement, prior, computation, and evidence. |
| Before the next week | Revisit the previous module only enough to state the central idea without looking. | A one-sentence bridge to the next topic. |

## The Five Questions

For every method in the course, classical or learned, ask:

1. What is measured?
2. What is unknown?
3. What forward process connects them?
4. What prior, regularizer, or learned assumption is added?
5. What could fail, even if the image looks plausible?

These questions are more important than memorizing code. The code is there to test the model.

## A Minimal 30-Minute Preparation

When time is short before class:

1. Read the first page or introduction of the book chapter.
2. Look at the two-session plan slide in the corresponding deck.
3. Find the main formula or model.
4. Write one question about what the model assumes.
5. Check whether the week has a quiz, project milestone, or special constraint.

This is enough to enter the class discussion without treating the materials as a pile of disconnected files.

## Quiz Weeks

Weeks 4 and 12 reserve the final 20 minutes of the second meeting for quizzes. On those weeks:

- use the [quiz information page](quizzes.qmd) to check timing and permitted tools;
- review the practice prompts from the preceding material;
- focus on explaining concepts and models without code;
- remember that only a basic calculator is permitted unless the instructor explicitly announces a change.

Actual quiz questions and solution keys are not published on this website before the quizzes.

## Project Weeks

The project should grow from the same weekly rhythm. Each project should be able to answer:

- What is the forward model?
- What is the reconstruction method?
- What evidence supports the result?
- What parameter or modeling choice matters?
- What failure case did the team test?
- What role did the LLM play, and what did the students verify themselves?

Use the [project page](project/index.qmd) for the formal project description and rubric. The proposal, report, and oral defense remain the assessed moments specified by the syllabus.

## LLM Use While Studying

LLMs can be useful for explanation, debugging, and alternative examples, but they should not replace the student's own model of the problem. A good use pattern is:

1. try the notebook or derivation yourself;
2. ask the LLM to explain one obstacle;
3. verify the answer against the book, slides, notebook output, or instructor guidance;
4. write the final understanding in your own words.

LLM assistance is not permitted during quizzes. For the project, LLM use is required but ownership is checked through the report, code, evidence, LLM-use statement, and individual oral defense.
